chapters.title: Push past daily resistance
chapters.text:
Success is not handed to you. Plenty of odds line up against you. Right in the center of those odds sits resistance, the wall that keeps us from getting work done. Resistance is a pushing force that shoves us away, pulls our attention off course, and chips away at our progress.

Resistance is the parent of stalling. It nudges us to delay the work that matters most. It is an opponent you have to beat if you want a full and useful life. Its grip on your output cannot be brushed off. You have to study it, learn what wakes it up, and steer it aside so you can finish any project.

Resistance, peers, relatives, and plain practical reasoning all throw obstacles in the path of growth.

Many forces try to stop you from doing things, but those forces only hold power over people who hand it to them. Anyone, and we mean anyone, can pick up their work and move on to bigger projects. The question is how. This summary answers that. You will learn how to take control of the forces of failure instead of being trapped by them. You do not have to be a prisoner of failure, because you are strong enough to beat it. You just need the right tools. This advice will help with any creative project, whether you are a writer, a dancer, or an entrepreneur. No matter your goal, if you want to reach a better state and live up to your potential, jump in. We will go from start to finish, but with a fresh angle, the angle of resistance itself.

A note for readers: the bigger your task is, the more resistance you will feel while trying to finish it.

chapters.title: The forces that block work
chapters.text:
As creatives, artists, and entrepreneurs, we face daily distractions that often keep us from working well. These forces take many forms, and the first is resistance.

Resistance is a common enemy that keeps us from taking needed action. Anxiety, feelings of being less than others, inaction, dependence on others, distraction, shyness, selfishness, and obsession are common ways it shows up. Activities that stir resistance give up instant pleasure in exchange for steady output, well-being, or good behavior. Resistance shows up in our eating plans, daily workouts, personal growth, and skills training.

The traits of resistance are these. It is invisible. You can only feel it as an energy flow from work in progress. It is impersonal. It is a force of nature, and it acts objectively. It is sneaky. It will lie, make things up, charm, scare, and push you into ignoring your tasks. It is always effective. It always gets into your head. It is constant. You have to fight a new battle with it every day. It aims to kill. It does not want to hurt or weaken. It wants to end things.

Unchecked raw emotion is the second enemy of creativity, after resistance.

Sometimes we make mistakes by listening too much to rational thoughts. We think they help, but since they are self-centered beliefs, they only lead to bad results. Instead, we need to tap into our unconscious mind, not our ego.

Friends and family can also get in the way of our productivity and our wish to change, because they want us to stay the same, which hurts success.

The odds are not entirely against us. These factors are on our side. First, plain ignorance and even stupidity can help in unusual cases. A creative person has to be confident enough to take on a task while ignoring how hard and demanding it is. Stop overthinking. Just do it. Second, stubbornness beats perseverance. When we are stubborn, quitting does not even cross our minds. Third, blind faith plays a big part in creativity and productivity. Believing in things you cannot see can help. Despite its best efforts, do not let resistance shake that faith. Fourth, passion makes us brilliant at whatever we do. Sometimes fear covers up our love and we think we have lost it, but that is often not true. Fifth, friends and family are there for us. When you are unsure what to do next, lean on the people close to you.

chapters.title: Start before you feel ready
chapters.text:
The lack of a solid plan or the challenge of taking on a project is not why we fail. The enemy of success is the noise in our head that keeps stopping us from reaching our goal. A nagging voice tells us why we cannot do a task or start a project. The problem with that voice is that it hides well, so it is hard to notice. We have to admit it is there before we can find ways to silence it.

It is also important to avoid getting caught in endless research. Learning new things about a topic matters, but we have to be careful not to cross the line, because too much research can become a wall. Just a quick search, no notes, no underlining, no more thinking or talking about the papers. Let the ideas grow on their own.

Let your subconscious do its work. You can always go back and do a deep study after you have started moving.

Creative art grows from a primal place. New ideas usually start at that raw level.

Yes, it is better to be simpleminded than overcomplicated. A bit of intentional stupidity is sometimes better than fake intelligence. We cannot work slowly and reluctantly if we want to do big things. We have to go for the kill right away, or we will not be able to do anything meaningful.

The lesson is simple. Move fast and on instinct. When you start to doubt and feel like giving up, look at what you have already done and let that push you to do more.

We often think that if we practice enough, we will be so perfect that we will never have problems with our work, which is not true. It is fine to feel worried or afraid, but try not to let those feelings run the show. When you are scared, keep going. That is the real test of your strength.

When it comes to writing, get the outline of your piece onto one sheet of paper. That way, you will not get stuck in the small details. You can see what to work on next instead of spending hours inventing every side character's history.

Another trick is to work backward. Decide where you want to end up and then figure out how to get there.

Whatever you can do or dream you can, begin it. Boldness has genius, power, and magic in it, a line often linked to Steven Pressfield.

chapters.title: Do not underestimate resistance on your path
chapters.text:
Let us move on to the next step of the project. During this stage, you fill in the gaps in the outline. You can start your research now.

What you put into those gaps is your pitch. It helps to be bold and let ideas flow. For example, the movie The Hangover has three acts: lose Doug, search for him, find him. When you fill in that outline, you get Stu's wedding with a dancer, Mike Tyson and his tiger, and the high-stakes run-in with Mr. Chow. Those scenes grip the viewer and feel exciting.

Resistance matters a lot at this stage. We have to notice it and respect it, accepting that we can never wipe it out, but we can outsmart it. Outwitting resistance takes the right mindset, especially one that is just as strong.

We need to stay aware of how our desires shape our lives. Simply put, do not stress over quality. Sometimes hold back from overthinking, because momentum matters. Putting our inner voice to rest and moving forward without fear is key to becoming comfortable with hard tasks. Just make sure you finish something, even if it is rough or imperfect.

It is also important to understand that ideas do not always come in a clean line. Creative ideas do not usually follow neat logic. We will likely find the plan we want somewhere in the middle or the end of our work. Instead of fighting that, be ready for it.

You can always carry a journal or a recorder. Capturing ideas helps because you have something to refer back to when you forget.

Playing back a recording and hearing yourself talk about a great idea you had missed is a huge relief.

There are two steps in the writing, creating, or idea-building process: action and reflection. Action means putting words down, and reflection means looking at your notes or sketches. Never try to do both at the same time.

Our job is to spot our concept before bringing it to life, not to control every idea that shows up. Our job is to listen for that resonance and find the idea. Ignore the doubts and deliver the concept anyway.

chapters.title: The seven principles of resistance
chapters.text:
At this point, you are about halfway through your project. This stage is tough because you will likely hit a wall that feels unbreakable. To break through, you need to master seven principles of resistance.

First, there is an enemy. Society tries to brainwash us into thinking evil is not real. Many of us believe the gloom we feel around us and inside us is just an illusion. That is not true. There is a clear adversary working against us. Recognizing that fact is the first step to getting to the other side.

Second, resistance is unstoppable. It has a killing intent, like a serious illness. We have to accept that our enemy is clever, adaptable, stubborn, brutal, and devastating.

Third, resistance lives inside us. It is a primary foe. It does not come from managers, competitors, or anyone we see as an enemy. It is our own creation. We carry it in our subconscious.

Fourth, we are not the resistance. The chatter and noise in our heads are not our fault.

To beat resistance, we have to spot its triggers first.

Fifth, we have to fight resistance. Imagine resistance is a wild animal. We cannot be nice to it, and we cannot try to negotiate with it. The fight is the only way.

Sixth, resistance comes second. Before resistance comes the idea. If we can build a vision strong enough to stir up resistance in us, we can beat the negative voice in our head. Help is the opposite of resistance. Help gives us the push we need to keep going, while resistance gives us many reasons to stop.

Whenever you are in doubt, ask yourself two questions. How badly do I want this? Why do I want it? By the time you answer, you should have found a way to recharge and face the task.

Remember and reignite your passion for the project. Find as many reasons to keep going as you can. Whether your motivation is fame, money, a vision, or the wish to prove yourself, hold on to it to kill the resistance.

A note for readers: in most myths, the hero has a helper, the force of assistance. In Native American stories, that helper is often a totemic animal.

chapters.title: Do not let fear win
chapters.text:
The one thing you can count on is the Big Crash, the one big thing that goes wrong. It should not make you quit. Now you have to go back to being a little stupid, a little stubborn, and a little blind in your faith.

Learn not to take failures or wins personally. Crashes are your chance to look back and grow. Solve the problem and move on. No mistake defines your value or worth.

Many people get tangled in fear. We often fear success so much that we steer clear of the very things that would help us reach it. That trap is resistance's favorite game.

Most of us live two lives: the life we live and the unlived life inside us. Between the two stands resistance, a line often linked to Steven Pressfield.

Resistance taints every corner of our lives in ways we cannot see or admit. Its trick is to make us feel exposed and naked. It makes us feel exposed and fools us into believing that if we open up, we will be judged.

Nobody wants to be judged or pushed away, so we get scared and let that fear cover up how important the work is. As a result, we do not finish the task. Resistance wins.

We wreck our own success when we let resistance grow and take over our thoughts and actions.

You can stop that outcome. Beat resistance at its own game, and you will be free, because once you have beaten it, you know you can do it again. Finish something once, and you will handle that task a million times more easily. No matter how often you stumble, keep moving until you reach your goal. You can get it right as long as you keep checking where you stand. What matters is that you have beaten the enemy and built a new life. You become different, and finishing tasks gets much easier.

Still, do not let your guard down. Remember, resistance never fully goes away. Sooner or later, you will hear its voice in your head. So be ready. And when resistance shows up again, you will have a few tricks ready. Most of all, start again before you feel ready.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
Take a clear look in the mirror. Will you allow resistance to drag you off your path, or will you set fear down and do the work?

You are an artistic and creative soul, a real one. The innovator inside you needs a spark to take action. No matter what storms are swirling around you, you have to keep your eyes on your targets. A silent force is trying to push you back, scatter your focus, and keep you from your craft. Pour everything you have into making sure that force does not win. Your success rides on it.

One thing every successful person shares is hunger. They attack each task with every ounce of energy they can gather. They ignore the soft voice of resistance whispering in their head. They act. Train yourself to act the same way.

Whatever project has been sitting on your shelf, pick it up and start right now, this very moment. Begin with the rough sketch of an idea. There will be time later to polish and fix. Stay open to other voices and learn from people who have walked the road. Shared knowledge cuts through any emotional wall that tries to choke your creativity.

Try this simple exercise. On any project, slice your outline into three acts: the opening, the middle, and the close. A great example sits in Leonardo da Vinci's The Last Supper, which breaks into the table that fills the canvas, the gathering of Jesus and his followers, and the play of perspective lines.

Cutting your work into acts shrinks the fear factor. You can also pin your theme to a sticky note on the wall to keep every scene lined up with that theme.

Read it through to the end?
